perturbation.original_text: Bills not paid when due shall accrue extended payment charges from the day following the date on which payment was due in accordance with Subparagraph 14.2 until the day on which it is paid. For the purposes of this Agreement, extended payment charges shall be computed at a rate equal to 150 % of the appropriate financial charges as indicated in Subparagraph 14.3 on the day following the date payment of the bill was due. In the event that applicable law allows the imposition of extended payment interest charges only at a rate less than that established in accordance with this Subparagraph, extended payment charges shall be at the highest rate permitted by such applicable law. In this case, appropriate documents to demonstrate the applicability of such law shall be provided by the concerned Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Bills not paid when due shall accrue extended payment charges from the day following the date on which payment was due in accordance with Subparagraph 14.2 until the day on which it is paid. For the purposes of this Agreement, extended payment charges shall be computed at a rate equal to 300% of the appropriate financial charges as indicated in Subparagraph 14.3 on the day following the date payment of the bill was due. In the event that applicable law allows the imposition of extended payment interest charges only at a rate less than that established in accordance with this Subparagraph, extended payment charges shall be at the highest rate permitted by such applicable law. In this case, appropriate documents to demonstrate the applicability of such law shall be provided by the concerned Party.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the late payment charge to 300% introduces a potential inconsistency as usury laws in many jurisdictions may limit the maximum interest rate that can be charged on debts. Setting it to 300% would likely make it unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have usury laws that limit the maximum interest rate that can be charged on debts. Charging 300% interest could violate these laws, making the clause unenforceable.

Rate of interest on loans, discounts and purchases Any association may take, receive, reserve, and charge on any loan or discount made, or upon any notes, bills of exchange, or other evidences of debt, interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, Territory, or District wh
perturbation.justification: The modified text increases the extended payment charge to 300% of the appropriate financial charges. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that many states have usury laws that limit the maximum interest rate that can be charged on debts, which this change would violate, making the clause unenforceable. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Title 12, Chapter 2, Subchapter IV, § 85, which addresses the rate of interest on loans and states that any association may charge interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, Territory, or District where the bank is located. A 300% charge would likely exceed what is allowed, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In calling the General Committee meetings, the chairperson shall give at least forty-five (45) days' advance notice of each meeting together with a copy of the draft agenda. In cases of emergency, such notice period may be reduced if Parties representing at least one-third (1/3) of the total voting interests as specified in Schedule B, are in agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In calling the General Committee meetings, the chairperson shall give at least five (5) days' advance notice of each meeting together with a copy of the draft agenda. In cases of emergency, such notice period may be reduced if Parties representing at least one-third (1/3) of the total voting interests as specified in Schedule B, are in agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the advance notice to five (5) days introduces a potential inconsistency, as some corporate governance laws or best practices suggest longer notification periods for significant meetings to allow adequate preparation by participants.  This may cause issues with adequate representation.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware General Corporation Law, Title 8, Section 222
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, widely used for corporate governance, requires reasonable notice for meetings. Five days might be deemed insufficient for significant decisions, potentially leading to legal challenges regarding the validity of decisions made at such meetings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 211 Â§ 212 Â§ 213 Â§ 214 Â§ 215 Â§ 216 Â§ 217 Â§ 218 Â§ 219 Â§ 220 Â§ 221 Â§ 222 Â§ 223 Â§ 224 Â§ 225 Â§ 226 Â§ 227 Â§ 228 Â§ 229 Â§ 230 Â§ 231 Â§ 232 Â§ 233 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meeting substantially concurrently with such proceedings, and (iii) if any stockholder or proxyholder votes or takes o
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the advance notice for General Committee meetings from forty-five (45) days to five (5) days. The Law Explanation indicates that Delaware law requires 'reasonable notice' for meetings, and five days might be deemed insufficient for significant decisions, potentially leading to legal challenges. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Delaware Code Title 8, Chapter 1, Subchapter VII, Â§ 211, addresses meetings of stockholders. While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable notice,' it implies that stockholders must have an adequate opportunity to participate and vote. Reducing the notice period to five days could impede stockholders' ability to adequately prepare, potentially contradicting the implicit requirement for reasonable notice under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall remain bound by the provisions of this Paragraph 29 during the period of this Agreement and for the period of five years following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall remain bound by the provisions of this Paragraph 29 during the period of this Agreement and for the period of one year following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality period to one year following termination may contradict reasonable business expectations and trade secret laws. If the confidential information has a commercially valuable life beyond one year, this shortened period may be insufficient to protect it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law - Duration of Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secrets law protects confidential information for as long as it remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year, regardless of the information's ongoing value and secrecy, could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, especially if the information remains a trade secret after that period.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year following termination of the agreement. This creates an inconsistency with trade secret law, which protects confidential information for as long as it remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. The Law Explanation states that limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable if the information remains a trade secret after that period. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that if the confidential information has a commercially valuable life beyond one year, this shortened period may be insufficient to protect it, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition.
